query: diagnostic value of radiography in acute alveolar abscess

retrieved_docs.title: Dental Emergencies -- Evaluation -- Infectious Dental Emergencies
retrieved_docs.content: Dental radiographs can show the extent of the infection and help evaluate areas that are not obvious to visual evaluation. These include intraoral periapical radiographs, bitewing radiographs, and panoramic radiographs. These radiographs help to identify the extent of dental caries, the presence of a periapical abscess, alveolar bone loss concerning periodontitis, and third molar eruptions. The dental practitioner’s physical examination must confirm all radiographic evidence for a full evaluation and accurate diagnosis. CT scans and MRIs help assess deep-space infections and osteomyelitis. Cone-beam CT is becoming increasingly common in clinical settings to better visualize areas of oral infections. [54]

retrieved_docs.title: Dental Abscess -- Evaluation
retrieved_docs.content: CT and MRI are sensitive modalities in detecting abscesses. Initial workup should include an x-ray of the head and neck and a complete blood cell count. X-rays of the head and neck can help identify compression or deviation of the trachea and subcutaneous air related to necrotic tissue. These imaging modalities can be useful if there is a need to evaluate possible ascending bacterial spread to the sinus cavities. A complete blood cell count can help rule out infectious etiology based on the total white blood cell count and the predominant white blood cell line that appears. There should be an increase in neutrophil count for bacterial infections in individuals who are not immunocompromised.

retrieved_docs.title: Oral Facial Infection of Dental Origin: A Guide for the Medical Practitioner -- Evaluation -- Diagnostic Imaging
retrieved_docs.content: Imaging becomes necessary when deep-space infection, abscess formation, or unusual progression is suspected. A panoramic radiograph (orthopantomogram) helps identify periapical lesions, impacted teeth, or osseous involvement. Periapical radiographs allow for detailed assessment of individual teeth. Contrast-enhanced computed tomography imaging serves as the gold standard for evaluating deep-space involvement and distinguishing cellulitis from abscess. Ultrasound offers a noninvasive method for detecting superficial abscesses, especially in the submandibular or buccal regions.

retrieved_docs.title: Alveolar Osteitis -- Differential Diagnosis
retrieved_docs.content: The primary differential diagnosis that needs to be considered is an infection, mainly if there are signs of disease beyond the extraction socket. Symptoms consistent with alveolar osteitis presenting or persisting weeks after surgery are concerning for acute osteomyelitis, subperiosteal infection, or bony sequestra formation. Based on the clinical evaluation of the patient, it may be appropriate to obtain a panoramic radiograph to evaluate healing.

retrieved_docs.title: Periodontal Abscess -- Prognosis
retrieved_docs.content: Periodontal abscesses characteristically demonstrate the rapid destruction of the periodontal ligament and alveolar bone. They can significantly influence the prognosis of a tooth and may lead to tooth loss; this is particularly true for patients who already have moderate to severe attachment loss.

retrieved_docs.title: Chronic Aspiration -- Evaluation -- Radiographic Evidence
retrieved_docs.content: Further complications of these sequelae include lung abscess and empyema. It is important to differentiate lung abscess and empyema as they are treated drastically differently. Plain films of a lung abscess demonstrate air-fluid levels in a generally round cavity, while empyema is usually lentiform. Distinguishing features are more readily observable on CT. Abscesses have thick irregular walls and form an acute angle with the pleura. In contrast, empyemas have smooth walls and form an obtuse angle with the pleura. In addition, abscesses abruptly interrupt bronchovascular structures, while empyemas compress and distort adjacent lung tissue, often causing separation and thickening of visceral and parietal pleura. [23]

retrieved_docs.title: Periodontal Abscess -- Evaluation
retrieved_docs.content: A periapical radiolucency is usually an indication of an endodontic abscess or a combination of a periodontal and endodontic abscess. The latter often demonstrates characteristic alveolar bone loss extending to the periapical lesions.

retrieved_docs.title: Lung Abscess -- Evaluation
retrieved_docs.content: Chest radiographs and CT aid in the diagnosis of a lung abscess. Differentiating between lung abscesses and pulmonary cystic lesions, including intrapulmonary bronchial cysts, sequestration, or secondary infected emphysematous bullae, can be challenging. However, the lesion's location and clinical signs often guide the diagnosis. Localized pleural empyema can be distinguished using a CT scan ( Image . Computed Tomography of a Lung Abscess) or ultrasound. The radiological sign of an air-fluid level can also be seen in hydatid cysts of the lung. [10]

retrieved_docs.title: Actinomycosis -- Differential Diagnosis
retrieved_docs.content: The differential will depend upon the site of infection. It is a slow-spreading infection that can mimic benign or malignant tumors, nocardiosis, and TB. The other important differential diagnosis to consider are the following: Aspiration pneumonia Appendicitis Diverticulosis Crohn disease Lung abscess Fungal pneumonia Pelvic inflammatory disease Brain abscess Dental abscess

retrieved_docs.title: Periodontal Abscess -- Evaluation
retrieved_docs.content: In some particular cases, darkfield microscopic examination of the abscess is used to rule out a periapical abscess due to the difference in the microflora. Positron emission tomography has high accuracy in detecting periodontal and other anaerobic abscesses in the oral cavity. [1]

retrieved_docs.title: Periodontal Abscess -- Differential Diagnosis
retrieved_docs.content: Partial root fracture: fractures are detectable through visual inspection, increased mobility, and tenderness. Taking radiographs from different angles can help to detect fractures. Similarly, endodontic perforations or perforations resulting from posts are also detectable through intraoral radiographs of alternative angulations. [3]

retrieved_docs.title: Alveolar Osteitis -- Evaluation
retrieved_docs.content: An increase in pain intensity a few days after a tooth extraction is the key feature. The absence of a blood clot and moderate to severe pain is pathognomonic of alveolar osteitis, and additional laboratory and radiographic studies are not required. As alveolar osteitis is not an infection but a delayed healing process, no leukocytosis would be expected.

retrieved_docs.title: Empyema(Archived) -- Evaluation
retrieved_docs.content: Due to the lack of specificity in a clinical presentation, more tests are needed to establish a diagnosis. [8]

retrieved_docs.title: Subphrenic Abscess -- Evaluation
retrieved_docs.content: Leukocytosis with neutrophilia and an elevated sedimentation rate are common. A chest x-ray may show hemidiaphragm elevation, pleural effusion, and lung abnormalities. Blood cultures indicating polymicrobial bacteria are highly suggestive of a subphrenic abscess. Abdominal ultrasound is the gold standard for right-sided subphrenic abscesses, with high sensitivity for fluid collections. A computerized tomography (CT) scan seems better for detecting a left-sided subphrenic abscess. CT scan is the diagnostic imaging of any patient with suspected intraabdominal abscess. [7] Sagittal imaging on a CT scan is best for visualizing subdiaphragmatic abscesses. Abdominal magnetic resonance imaging (MRI) and an indium-111-labeled leukocyte scan may be helpful for hidden intraabdominal or subphrenic abscesses. MR enterography is the investigation of choice in patients with inflammatory bowel disease. Blood gas analysis may reveal respiratory alkalosis initially due to hyperventilation and may progress to metabolic acidosis if left untreated.

retrieved_docs.title: Periodontal Abscess -- Evaluation
retrieved_docs.content: Periapical radiographs are critical in evaluating the periodontal hard tissues. Widening of the periodontal ligaments and horizontal or vertical bone loss is often expected.

retrieved_docs.title: Actinomycosis -- Evaluation -- Cervicofacial
retrieved_docs.content: Imaging findings are nonspecific and are noncontributory in diagnosing the disease but will help in assessing the degree of soft tissue and bone involvement. Dental radiographs are helpful in evaluating the apical abscesses. CT and MRI studies may show osteolysis in the chronic form of infection.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 12 -- Level 3 -- Community 2159
retrieved_docs.content: Computed tomography of the chest revealing a miliary pulmonary infiltrate is a radiologic finding that occurs in the lungs.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 7 -- Level 3 -- Community 2659
retrieved_docs.content: Abces van long en mediastinum is identified as a finding located in the lung, indicating the presence of an abscess that may extend into the mediastinum. Bronchoscopy with drainage of lung abscess is a therapeutic procedure that, while performed via the airway, has an indirect procedural site in the lung, reflecting its role in managing pulmonary abscesses. Computed tomography of chest: miliary pulmonary infiltrate also represents a finding in the lung, revealing a diffuse pattern of tiny nodular infiltrates that suggest widespread pulmonary involvement. Together, these entities underscore the lung as the central anatomic site for both diagnostic findings (abscess and miliary infiltrates) and interventional management (bronchoscopic drainage) of serious pulmonary infections.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 27 -- Level 2 -- Community 1217
retrieved_docs.content: Abces van long en mediastinum, a form of خراج (abscess formation) that belongs to the category of other lower respiratory disease, is linked to اضطراب منصفي (segmental disorder) and frequently manifests as كتلة منصفية (mediastinal mass) within the mediastinum. The mediastinum, situated in the Subdivision of thorax, serves as a common anatomical site for direct procedural findings on thoracic CT imaging, including mediastinal‑pleural fistulas that may be unilateral (right or left), bilateral, or localized. Computed tomography of the chest (ct scan chest) is the primary diagnostic modality for detecting a spectrum of mediastinal and pulmonary abnormalities such as mass of the mid mediastinum, mass of the posterior mediastinum, mass of the superior mediastinum, general mediastinal mass, mediastinal mass containing calcium, mediastinal effusion, and miliary pulmonary infiltrate. Mediastinal effusion is classified as a type of اضطراب منصفي. Various CT techniques are employed, ranging from CT angiography of thorax with contrast (a specific ct scan chest isa) to diagnostic thoracic CT without contrast, highlighting the breadth of imaging options. Findings are reported through computed tomography, thorax, diagnostic; without contrast material and related ct scan chest subtypes. Therapeutically, closed drainage of chest is performed within the thoracic anatomical framework to manage these pathologies. Collectively, these relationships illustrate how lower respiratory diseases, particularly those involving abscesses, can involve the mediastinum; how mediastinal pathology—including masses, effusions, calcifications, diffuse infiltrates, and mediastinal‑pleural communications—is visualized and classified through diverse CT modalities; and how procedural interventions such as closed chest drainage are integrated into patient care.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 7 -- Level 2 -- Community 1213
retrieved_docs.content: Other lower respiratory disease is associated with خراج (abscess) and is classified as a subtype of اضطراب منصفي. The imaging finding described as computed tomography of chest: mediastinal effusion is also identified as an instance of اضطراب منصفي. Consequently, both the clinical entity other lower respiratory disease and the radiologic observation of mediastinal effusion share the same broader disorder category, اضطراب منصفي, while the respiratory disease specifically carries a risk of developing an abscess.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 24 -- Level 3 -- Community 2425
retrieved_docs.content: Computed tomography, thorax, diagnostic; without contrast material and Computerized axial tomography of thorax with contrast are both specific forms of a ct scan chest. The non‑contrast diagnostic CT of the thorax represents a narrower variant of the general ct scan chest procedure, while the contrast‑enhanced axial tomography is classified as a type of ct scan chest. Chest CT imaging is employed to evaluate intrathoracic pathology, and it can reveal findings such as a mass of the mid mediastinum (computed tomography of chest: mass of mid mediastinum) and a miliary pulmonary infiltrate (computed tomography of chest: miliary pulmonary infiltrate). These relationships illustrate how different CT techniques are categorized under the broader ct scan chest modality and how they are used to detect distinct thoracic abnormalities.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 19 -- Level 2 -- Community 447
retrieved_docs.content: The mediastinum is a central thoracic subdivision that serves as the anatomic site for a spectrum of lower‑respiratory conditions classified under “other lower respiratory disease” (اضطراب منصفي), including mediastinal masses (كتلة منصفية), lung‑mediastinal abscesses, and mediastinal effusion. Computed tomography of the chest (ct scan chest) is the primary imaging modality for evaluating mediastinal pathology. Chest CT—performed as a diagnostic thoracic CT protocol that may begin without contrast and continue with contrast, or as CT angiography of the thorax with contrast—provides the “has_finding_method” link to a variety of mediastinal findings: mass of the posterior mediastinum, mass of the superior mediastinum, a general mediastinal mass, mediastinal mass containing calcium, and mediastinal effusion. Chest CT also identifies mediastinal‑pleural fistulas, recorded with the “has_direct_procedure_site” relationship to the mediastinum, and specifies the laterality of the fistula (bilateral, right‑sided, left‑sided). Therapeutically, closed drainage of the chest is associated with the thoracic subdivision and may be employed to manage complications such as mediastinal‑pleural fistulas. Together, the anatomical context of the mediastinum, its disease categorization, the detailed CT imaging techniques, the specific radiologic findings, and the related interventional procedures constitute an integrated framework for diagnosing and treating mediastinal and thoracic disorders.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 25 -- Level 3 -- Community 2713
retrieved_docs.content: CT angiography of thorax with contrast and Computerized axial tomography of thorax with contrast are specific types of chest CT scans. Diagnostic computed tomography of the thorax performed without contrast material, as well as diagnostic computed tomography of the thorax performed without contrast material followed by contrast material(s) and additional sections, represent narrower sub‑categories within the broader chest CT imaging modality. In addition, computed tomography of the chest is used as a finding method for detecting miliary pulmonary infiltrate. Together, these relationships delineate a hierarchical structure of chest CT techniques and illustrate how particular CT protocols are applied for detailed diagnostic evaluation of thoracic pathology.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 24 -- Level 1 -- Community 190
retrieved_docs.content: CT in identifying a wide range of mediastinal and pulmonary abnormalities. Collectively, these relationships delineate an integrated framework in which mediastinal pathology—ranging from abscesses, segmental disorders, and various mediastinal masses (including calcium‑bearing lesions) to pleural‑mediastinal fistulas and miliary infiltrates—is situated within the category of other lower respiratory disease and is primarily evaluated through chest CT imaging. The hierarchical structure links general CT scanning to specialized chest CT procedures, to specific diagnostic findings, while positioning conventional chest x‑ray as a related but subordinate imaging option, thereby supporting comprehensive assessment and management of mediastinal and lower‑respiratory conditions in clinical practice.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 13 -- Level 1 -- Community 64
retrieved_docs.content: Abces van long en mediastinum is classified as a specific form of other lower respiratory disease located in the mediastinum and is associated with خراج (abscess formation). Other lower respiratory disease is linked to اضطراب منصفي (segmental disorder) and, via an inverse‑isa relationship, is considered a type of كتلة منصفية (mediastinal mass). Computed tomography of the chest (ct scan chest) belongs to the broader category “إجراءات تصوير متعلقة بمنطقة الصدر والسبيل التنفسي” (imaging procedures related to the chest and respiratory tract) and includes several procedural variants: “Computed tomography, thorax, diagnostic; without contrast material,” its extended protocol with subsequent contrast, and “Computerized axial tomography of thorax with contrast.” These CT modalities are the primary imaging procedures used to evaluate mediastinal pathology. CT imaging directly targets the mediastinum, as shown by has_direct_procedure_site links to findings such as mediastinal‑pleural fistula, mediastinal‑pleural fistula bilaterally, mediastinal‑pleural fistula on the right, and mediastinal‑pleural fistula on the left. Chest CT also serves as the finding method for mediastinal abnormalities including computed tomography of chest: mediastinal effusion (classified as اضطراب منصفي), mediastinal mass, mediastinal mass containing calcium, as well as specific locations of masses (mid, posterior, superior mediastinum). The parent imaging hierarchy also includes “صورة وعائية رئوية” (pulmonary angiographic image) as a complementary procedure. Together, these relationships illustrate that lower respiratory diseases such as lung‑mediastinal abscesses can present as mediastinal masses and complications like pleural‑mediastinal fistulas, and that a range of CT chest techniques—both non‑contrast and contrast‑enhanced—are essential for locating, characterizing, and managing these conditions, supporting accurate diagnosis and optimal patient care.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 8 -- Level 1 -- Community 162
retrieved_docs.content: The community describes a subtype of lower respiratory disease—lung and mediastinal abscess (Abces van long en mediastinum)—located in the mediastinum and characterized as a segmental disorder (اضطراب منصفي) associated with abscess (خراج). This condition is inversely related to mediastinal mass (كتلة منصفية), suggesting that mediastinal masses may represent manifestations of lower respiratory pathology. Chest imaging is organized under the category ct scan chest, which includes CT angiography of the thorax with contrast, non‑contrast thoracic CT, and contrast‑enhanced thoracic CT as narrower subtypes. Computerized axial tomography (CT) scan is a child of التصوير المقطعي المحوسب للصدر (CT of the chest), and both are parented by other chest x‑ray, reflecting overlapping categorization. ct scan chest is the primary finding method for a spectrum of mediastinal abnormalities identified on thoracic CT, including mediastinal masses of the superior, mid, and posterior mediastinum, mediastinal mass containing calcium, and mediastinal effusion, all classified as كتلة منصفية or اضطراب منصفي. The modality also serves as the diagnostic label for mediastinal‑pleural fistulas (right, left, bilateral). These relationships illustrate how chest CT imaging is central to detecting and characterizing mediastinal abscesses, segmental disorders, masses, effusions, and fistulous communications, thereby guiding patient evaluation and management.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 5 -- Level 1 -- Community 213
retrieved_docs.content: The community describes lung and mediastinal abscess (Abces van long en mediastinum) as a subtype of other lower respiratory disease, which is categorized under اضطراب منصفي and inversely linked to كتلة منصفية (mediastinal mass). Chest computed tomography (CT), including non‑contrast thoracic CT, CT of the chest (التصوير المقطعي المحوسب للصدر), and contrast‑enhanced thoracic CT, is identified as the primary diagnostic method for a range of mediastinal abnormalities. These imaging procedures, organized under the broader Computerized Axial Tomography (CT) hierarchy and related to other chest‑x‑ray modalities, reveal findings such as mediastinal masses (mid mediastinum, posterior mediastinum, superior mediastinum, calcium‑containing mass), mediastinal effusion (classified as اضطراب منصفي), and mediastinal‑pleural fistulas (right‑sided, left‑sided, bilateral, or unspecified). The relationships delineate how specific respiratory infections, anatomical locations, and imaging techniques interrelate in the evaluation and management of mediastinal and lower respiratory tract conditions.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 14 -- Level 1 -- Community 213
retrieved_docs.content: An abscess of the lung and mediastinum (خراج) is classified as an other lower respiratory disease, which is associated with segmental disorder (اضطراب منصفي) and inversely related to segmental mass (كتلة منصفية). The mediastinum, a central compartment of the chest (hrudník), is encompassed by the broader thoracic subdivision, and the chest serves as the site for clinical entities such as pleurisy, pneumothorax, pulmonary collapse, as well as interventions like closed chest drainage. Computed tomography of the chest (ct scan chest) is linked to the chest, mediastinum, and thoracic subdivision and is the principal imaging modality for evaluating mediastinal pathology. Specific CT protocols—CT angiography of thorax with contrast, computerized axial tomography of thorax with contrast, diagnostic thoracic CT without contrast, and diagnostic thoracic CT with subsequent contrast—are narrower forms of ct scan chest. Chest CT (التصوير المقطعي المحوسب للصدر) is the Arabic equivalent of computerized axial tomography and is related to other chest x‑ray as a parent modality. Chest CT directly visualizes mediastinal‑pleural fistulas (right, left, or bilateral), mediastinal masses in the mid, posterior, or superior mediastinum, calcium‑containing mediastinal masses, and mediastinal effusion, all categorized as كتلة منصفية or اضطراب منصفي. The combination of pleurisy, pneumothorax, and pulmonary collapse is associated with the condition دبيلة, underscoring the clinical relevance of accurate mediastinal imaging for diagnosing complications and guiding patient management.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 29 -- Level 3 -- Community 2419
retrieved_docs.content: The diagnostic protocol termed “Computed tomography, thorax, diagnostic; without contrast material, followed by contrast material(s) and further sections” represents a more specific form of a chest CT scan, indicating that a non‑contrast thoracic CT is performed first and then followed by contrast‑enhanced imaging with additional sections. This refined chest CT technique is employed to evaluate thoracic pathology and serves as a method for detecting distinct radiographic findings. In particular, chest CT imaging can reveal a mass located in the mid mediastinum, as described by “computed tomography of chest: mass of mid mediastinum,” and can also identify a pattern of miliary pulmonary infiltrates, referred to as “computed tomography of chest: miliary pulmonary infiltrate.” Recognizing these specific findings on a detailed chest CT study aids clinicians in diagnosing mediastinal lesions and diffuse pulmonary disease, thereby informing appropriate patient management.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 12 -- Level 1 -- Community 165
retrieved_docs.content: Lung and mediastinal abscess (Abces van long en mediastinum) is described as a manifestation of other lower respiratory disease, which is linked to the segmental disorder اضطراب منصفي and characteristically presents with an abscess (خراج). The mediastinum serves as the anatomic site of this disease and the direct procedural target for thoracic computed tomography (CT) scans that evaluate mediastinal pathology, including mediastinal‑pleural fistulas (right‑sided, left‑sided, or bilateral) and mediastinal effusion (also classified as اضطراب منصفي). Chest CT modalities such as contrast‑enhanced CT angiography of the thorax and computerized axial tomography of the thorax with contrast are the primary imaging techniques for detecting and characterizing mediastinal abnormalities, including masses located in the superior, mid, or posterior mediastinal compartments, mediastinal masses (كتلة منصفية) that contain calcium, and mediastinal effusion. Computed tomography of the chest (CT scan chest) is categorized under “Imaging procedures related to the chest and respiratory tract,” which also encompasses related child procedures such as pulmonary angiographic imaging (صورة وعائية رئوية). Although CT of the breast is a CT‑based technique, it is primarily associated with breast radiography rather than thoracic evaluation. Overall, the framework links the disease entities (lung‑mediastinal abscess, other lower respiratory disease, segmental disorder), anatomical location (mediastinum), imaging modalities (thoracic CT, contrast‑enhanced chest CT, pulmonary angiography), and specific radiologic findings (mediastinal‑pleural fistulas, mediastinal masses, calcium‑containing masses, mediastinal effusion) into an integrated diagnostic pathway for thoracic and mediastinal conditions.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 0 -- Level 2 -- Community 837
retrieved_docs.content: Abces van long en mediastinum is classified as a subtype of other lower respiratory disease. The category other lower respiratory disease is associated with خراج (abscess) and is itself a subcategory of اضطراب منصفي. In addition, the imaging finding computed tomography of chest: mediastinal effusion is an instance of اضطراب منصفي. Thus, both the specific clinical entity Abces van long en mediastinum and the radiologic manifestation mediastinal effusion are encompassed within the broader disorder اضطراب منصفي, highlighting the link between lower respiratory infections, abscess formation, and mediastinal pathology.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 11 -- Level 1 -- Community 165
retrieved_docs.content: Abces van long en mediastinum, classified as a subtype of other lower respiratory disease, is an infectious abscess (خراج) located in the mediastinum and is hierarchically linked to both اضطراب منصفي (mediastinal disorder) and كتلة منصفية (mediastinal mass). The mediastinum therefore serves as the primary anatomic site for a spectrum of lower‑respiratory pathologies that include mediastinal abscesses, mediastinal effusion (another form of اضطراب منصفي), and various mediastinal masses such as mid‑mediastinal, posterior‑mediastinal, superior‑mediastinal, generic mediastinal masses, and mediastinal masses containing calcium. All of these entities are identified and classified using computed tomography of the chest (ct scan chest), a thoracic CT protocol that also detects mediastinal‑pleural fistulas and their laterality variants (right‑sided, left‑sided, or bilateral). In addition, breast CT is grouped under the broader category of chest CT because it employs the same imaging technology. Collectively, these relationships illustrate that chest CT is the essential diagnostic platform for visualizing mediastinal effusions, diverse mediastinal masses (including calcified lesions), and mediastinal‑pleural fistulas, thereby guiding clinical decision‑making and procedural planning for mediastinal and lower respiratory disorders.
